items.q: How many layers are in the OSI model?
items.correct: 7 (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application).
items.why: Students confuse OSI (7 layers) with the TCP/IP model (4 layers). They are related but distinct - OSI is a theoretical reference model; TCP/IP is the practical Internet stack.

items.q: What is hexadecimal A in denary?
items.why: Students forget hex uses A-F to represent 10-15 because we need 16 distinct symbols. A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15.

items.q: What is the time complexity of binary search?
items.misconception: O(n) - because it searches through items.
items.why: Students conflate 'fast' with O(1). Binary search halves the search space each step, giving logarithmic time - much better than linear, but not constant.
Correct answer: variable values per step. “network packets” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's language-agnostic. “only Python” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: From x=5 loop above, total outputs printed?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
